overview: Bull's Head sits at a central Stamford crossroads where High Ridge Road, Long Ridge Road, and Route 104 converge. The intersection gives the neighborhood its commercial anchor — the Bull's Head Shopping Center and a cluster of local businesses — while the surrounding residential streets hold colonials, capes, and ranches on moderate lots. It is one of Stamford's most practical family neighborhoods: close to schools, ten minutes from Metro-North, and priced below the waterfront tiers.

children.text: The name comes from the intersection itself, a five-point junction that has served as a commercial waypoint since the colonial era. Bull's Head Shopping Center anchors the retail node, joined by local businesses like Bull's Head Market, a neighborhood deli that has been a fixture for decades, and the former Bull's Head Diner site on High Ridge Road where the restaurant DIG now operates. The commercial strip is not flashy. It is functional, and that matters when you live in a neighborhood where errands can be handled without driving to downtown or I-95.

Residential streets branch off the main roads into established blocks of mid-century and postwar housing. Three-bedroom colonials on quarter-acre lots dominate the inventory, interspersed with Cape Cod homes from the 1940s and 1950s and ranch-style houses from the 1960s. Updated kitchens and finished basements are common renovation targets. A few newer builds have appeared on subdivided lots, but the streetscape remains largely consistent with its original character. Mature oaks and maples line the sidewalks in the older sections.

Families with school-age children account for a significant share of buyer demand. The neighborhood feeds into Stamford's public school system, and its central location means neither Westhill High School nor Stamford High School is far. Scalzi Park, roughly a mile south on Bridge Street, provides ball fields, tennis courts, and a community pool that serves as a summer gathering point. The Stamford Museum & Nature Center sits a short drive north on Scofieldtown Road, adding 118 acres of trails, a working farm, and a planetarium to the recreational mix.

The ten-minute drive to Stamford's Metro-North station keeps the neighborhood relevant for commuters, though the connection requires a car rather than a walk. High Ridge Road runs south to the Stamford Town Center area and the train station; northbound, it leads to the Merritt Parkway interchange. Route 104 connects west toward North Stamford and east toward Newfield. Bull's Head will never be the glamour listing of Stamford real estate. It is the neighborhood where families build equity in solid housing stock at prices that still make mathematical sense relative to income.

highlights: Central crossroads of High Ridge Road, Long Ridge Road, and Route 104 with Bull's Head Shopping Center
Predominantly colonials, capes, and ranches on moderate lots priced between $550K and $850K
Scalzi Park provides ball fields, tennis courts, and a community pool roughly one mile south
Ten-minute drive to Stamford Metro-North station with express trains to Grand Central Terminal
Nearby Scofieldtown Road leads to the Stamford Museum & Nature Center with 118 acres of trails and Heckscher Farm
housingCharacteristics:
Bull's Head is defined by mid-century housing stock on moderate lots. Three-bedroom colonials, Cape Cod homes from the 1940s and 1950s, and ranch-style houses from the 1960s make up the majority of available inventory. Lot sizes typically range from a quarter-acre to a third of an acre, with occasional larger parcels along the north-south corridors. Many homes have been updated with renovated kitchens, hardwood floor refinishing, and finished lower levels.

Pricing runs from approximately $550,000 for smaller capes and ranches to $850,000 for larger colonials with updated systems and expanded square footage. Relative to Stamford's waterfront neighborhoods and North Stamford's estate-zoned properties, Bull's Head represents the city's middle market — solid construction, established trees, and prices that attract first-time buyers stepping up from condominiums as well as families seeking more space than downtown offers.
marketNotes:
Bull's Head trades on value and practicality. Buyer demand comes primarily from families relocating within Stamford — often moving out of downtown rentals or Harbor Point condos — and from households priced out of the Cove or Shippan. Updated colonials with four bedrooms and renovated kitchens generate the strongest interest, particularly those on streets with sidewalks and mature landscaping.

Inventory turns over at a moderate pace. Properties priced correctly within the $550,000 to $750,000 band tend to move in under 30 days. Homes above $800,000 compete with listings in adjacent neighborhoods that offer larger lots or proximity to the train. The corporate employment base in downtown Stamford supports consistent demand; many Bull's Head buyers work at Charter Communications, Synchrony, or one of the smaller firms clustered along Tresser Boulevard.
locationAccess:
Bull's Head sits at the junction of three major Stamford roads. High Ridge Road runs north-south, connecting the neighborhood to downtown Stamford and the Merritt Parkway. Long Ridge Road extends northward toward New Canaan. Route 104 provides the east-west link. The Stamford Metro-North station is roughly ten minutes south by car via High Ridge Road, and I-95 interchanges are accessible in a similar timeframe via downtown surface streets.

The neighborhood's central position means most of Stamford is reachable within fifteen minutes. Downtown restaurants and offices are a short drive south. The Cove and Shippan coastline sits to the southeast. North Stamford's open spaces are ten minutes north. Westchester County and Greenwich are both accessible via the Merritt Parkway or I-95 without passing through downtown congestion.

seoDescription: Browse Bull's Head homes in Stamford CT — colonials, capes, and ranches priced $550K to $850K near schools and Metro-North. Contact Matt Caiola today.

faqs.question: What types of homes are in Bull's Head, Stamford?
faqs.answer: Bull's Head consists primarily of colonials, Cape Cod homes, and ranch-style houses built from the 1940s through the 1960s, set on quarter-acre to third-acre lots. Many have been updated with modern kitchens and refinished hardwood floors. Prices typically range from $550,000 for smaller capes and ranches to $850,000 for larger, fully renovated colonials. The neighborhood offers more house per dollar than Stamford's waterfront or downtown districts.

faqs.question: How far is Bull's Head from the Stamford train station?
faqs.answer: The Stamford Metro-North station is approximately a ten-minute drive south from Bull's Head via High Ridge Road. Express trains from that station reach Grand Central Terminal in about 50 minutes. Bull's Head also sits near the Merritt Parkway interchange, giving residents a second commuter corridor for driving to Westchester or other Connecticut destinations without using I-95.

faqs.question: What parks and recreation are near Bull's Head?
faqs.answer: Scalzi Park is roughly one mile south on Bridge Street and includes ball fields, tennis courts, and a community swimming pool that draws families throughout the summer. The Stamford Museum & Nature Center sits a short drive north on Scofieldtown Road, offering 118 acres of hiking trails, Heckscher Farm with heritage-breed animals, a planetarium, and rotating exhibitions in the Bendel Mansion galleries.
